From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1971 film by Alan Arkin

This article is about the 1971 film. For the 2011 film, see [Little Murder](/wiki/Little_Murder "Little Murder"). For the 2009 TV Series, see [Les Petits Meurtres d'Agatha Christie](/wiki/Les_Petits_Meurtres_d%27Agatha_Christie "Les Petits Meurtres d'Agatha Christie").

| Little Murders | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Directed by | [Alan Arkin](/wiki/Alan_Arkin "Alan Arkin") |
| Screenplay by | [Jules Feiffer](/wiki/Jules_Feiffer "Jules Feiffer") |
| Based on | *Little Murders*   by Jules Feiffer |
| Produced by | Jack Brodsky |
| Starring | [Elliott Gould](/wiki/Elliott_Gould "Elliott Gould") [Donald Sutherland](/wiki/Donald_Sutherland "Donald Sutherland") [Lou Jacobi](/wiki/Lou_Jacobi "Lou Jacobi") Alan Arkin [Marcia Rodd](/wiki/Marcia_Rodd "Marcia Rodd")  [Vincent Gardenia](/wiki/Vincent_Gardenia "Vincent Gardenia")  [Elizabeth Wilson](/wiki/Elizabeth_Wilson "Elizabeth Wilson")  [Jon Korkes](/wiki/Jon_Korkes "Jon Korkes") |
| Cinematography | [Gordon Willis](/wiki/Gordon_Willis "Gordon Willis") |
| Edited by | Howard Kuperman |
| Music by | Fred Kaz |
| Color process | [Color by Deluxe](/wiki/DeLuxe_Color "DeLuxe Color") |
| Production company | Brodsky-Gould Productions |
| Distributed by | [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox") |
| Release date | * February 9, 1971 (1971-02-09) |
| Running time | 108 minutes |
| Country | United States |
| Language | English |
| Budget | $1.34 million[[1]](#cite_note-1) |
| Box office | $1.5 million (rentals)[[2]](#cite_note-2) |

***Little Murders*** is a 1971 American [black comedy film](/wiki/Black_comedy_film "Black comedy film") directed by [Alan Arkin](/wiki/Alan_Arkin "Alan Arkin"), in his feature film directorial debut, and starring [Elliott Gould](/wiki/Elliott_Gould "Elliott Gould") and [Marcia Rodd](/wiki/Marcia_Rodd "Marcia Rodd").[[3]](#cite_note-Little_Murders-3) Based on the stage play of the same name by [Jules Feiffer](/wiki/Jules_Feiffer "Jules Feiffer"), it is the story of a woman, Patsy Newquist (Rodd), who brings home her boyfriend, Alfred Chamberlain (Gould), to meet her severely dysfunctional family amidst a series of random shootings, garbage strikes and electrical outages ravaging their [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") neighborhood.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Patsy Newquist is a 27-year-old interior designer who lives in a [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") that is rife with street crime, noise, obscene phone calls, power blackouts and unsolved homicides. When she sees a defenseless man being attacked by street thugs, she intervenes, but is surprised when the passive victim doesn't even bother to thank her. She ends up attracted to the man, Alfred Chamberlain, a photographer of excrement, but finds that he is emotionally vacant, barely able to feel pain or pleasure. He permits muggers to beat him up until they get tired and go away.

Patsy is accustomed to "molding" men. Alfred is different. When she brings him home to meet her parents Carol and Marjorie and her brother Kenny, he is almost non-verbal, except to tell her that he doesn't care for families. He learns that Patsy had another brother who was murdered for no known reason. Patsy's eccentric family is surprised when she announces their intention to wed, then amazed when their marriage ceremony conducted by the existential Rev. Henry Dupas turns into a mass fight. Determined to discover why her new husband is the way he is, Patsy sends Alfred to [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") to visit his parents, who he left at 17 years of age, with a questionnaire about his childhood. He records the interview with his parents, in which they deny memory of his childhood.

Alfred ultimately agrees to try to become Patsy's kind of man, the kind willing to "fight back," professes he is able to feel emotions again, and that his first feeling is worship for Patsy. The instant that happens, a sniper's bullet kills Patsy, again for no apparent reason. A blood-splattered Alfred goes to her parents' apartment, New Yorkers barely noticing his state. He descends into a silent stupor, Carol even having to feed him. A ranting, disturbed police detective, Lt. Miles Practice, drops by, almost unable to function due to the number of unsolved murders in the city. After he leaves, Alfred goes for a walk in the park, where he starts to take photographs of a statue and some kids. He returns with a rifle, which he doesn't know how to load. Carol shows him how. Then the two of them, along with Kenny, take turns shooting people down on the street. Upon believing Alfred to have shot Lt. Practice, their mood brightens, and all three men giddily join Marjorie for dinner at the table.

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

* [Elliott Gould](/wiki/Elliott_Gould "Elliott Gould") as Alfred Chamberlain
* [Marcia Rodd](/wiki/Marcia_Rodd "Marcia Rodd") as Patsy Newquist
* [Vincent Gardenia](/wiki/Vincent_Gardenia "Vincent Gardenia") as Carol Newquist
* [Elizabeth Wilson](/wiki/Elizabeth_Wilson "Elizabeth Wilson") as Marjorie Newquist
* [Jon Korkes](/wiki/Jon_Korkes "Jon Korkes") as Kenny Newquist
* [John Randolph](/wiki/John_Randolph_(actor) "John Randolph (actor)") as Mr. Chamberlain
* [Doris Roberts](/wiki/Doris_Roberts "Doris Roberts") as Mrs. Chamberlain
* [Donald Sutherland](/wiki/Donald_Sutherland "Donald Sutherland") as Reverend Henry Dupas
* [Lou Jacobi](/wiki/Lou_Jacobi "Lou Jacobi") as Judge Stern
* [Alan Arkin](/wiki/Alan_Arkin "Alan Arkin") as Lieutenant Miles Practice

## On stage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: On stage")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

[Jules Feiffer](/wiki/Jules_Feiffer "Jules Feiffer") says he was inspired to write the story by the [assassination of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy"). "Which was odd because I wasn't a big fan of his; he was the first actor in the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House")," he said. "And then when [Oswald](/wiki/Lee_Harvey_Oswald "Lee Harvey Oswald") was shot, I thought there is a madness going on. And because of my politics, I saw that madness in Vietnam, too. So the motive of the play was the breakdown of all forms of authority—religion, family, the police. Urban violence was always the metaphor in my mind for something more serious in the country."[[4]](#cite_note-Freedman-4)

Feiffer originally wrote the story as a novel. "Having gone to theater a lot and read plays a lot since adolescence, I realized that if I ever wrote the sort of play I wanted to write, it would close in a week. I felt I'd already done my masochistic years at *[The Village Voice](/wiki/The_Village_Voice "The Village Voice")*—eight years of cartoons without a penny. But I felt this grim sense of what was going on and I didn't feel the cartoons could express that fully. I also felt the cartoons were being too easily accepted."[[4]](#cite_note-Freedman-4)

Feiffer worked on the novel for two years but was unhappy with it.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Then he discovered an original outline for the novel which he thought would make a good play. He wrote a first draft in three weeks. "And I realized that whatever the fate of the play, I was stuck as a playwright. I felt as at home with a play as with the cartoon."[[4]](#cite_note-Freedman-4)

### 1967 Broadway production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1967 Broadway production")]

The play was going to have its world premiere at the [Yale School of Drama](/wiki/Yale_School_of_Drama "Yale School of Drama") in October 1966.[[6]](#cite_note-6) However that ended when there was a chance it would be produced on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre"). [Alexander Cohen](/wiki/Alexander_H._Cohen "Alexander H. Cohen") eventually got the rights.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Stage rights were also optioned by the [Aldwych Theatre](/wiki/Aldwych_Theatre "Aldwych Theatre") in [London](/wiki/London "London").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The play was staged on Broadway in 1967 with a cast that included [Elliott Gould](/wiki/Elliott_Gould "Elliott Gould"), [David Steinberg](/wiki/David_Steinberg "David Steinberg") and [Barbara Cook](/wiki/Barbara_Cook "Barbara Cook") and a budget of $100,000. It was directed by George L. Sherman.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Reviews were mixed. [Walter Kerr](/wiki/Walter_Kerr "Walter Kerr") said "The comedy comes to a point where it can no longer keep a grin on its face, not even a twisted one. Mr. Feiffer gives over the business of suggesting serious comment from inside a lazy, lunatic stance, and like a too successfully reformed gag man, goes straight."[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Feiffer called it an "atrocious production" but admits he was involved in all key creative decisions.[[13]](#cite_note-atrocious-13) The play lasted only seven performances.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Walter Kerr, who had given the play a mixed review, wrote an article saying the play had promise and that it was a shame the piece could not have been further developed.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The fate of the play was given as an example of the lack of critical and producer support given to new American plays on Broadway.[[13]](#cite_note-atrocious-13)

### 1967 London production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1967 London production")]

This failure was followed by a successful London production by the [Royal Shakespeare Company](/wiki/Royal_Shakespeare_Company "Royal Shakespeare Company"), directed by [Christopher Morahan](/wiki/Christopher_Morahan "Christopher Morahan") at the Aldwych Theatre. Reviews were better though not raves and the play was voted by critics as best foreign play of the year.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Feiffer said this production "saved my sanity."[[13]](#cite_note-atrocious-13)

### 1969 Off Broadway production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1969 Off Broadway production")]

[Theodore Mann](/wiki/Theodore_Mann "Theodore Mann") had the idea of reviving the play off Broadway and hired [Alan Arkin](/wiki/Alan_Arkin "Alan Arkin") to direct. It was revived in 1969 by [Circle in the Square](/wiki/Circle_in_the_Square "Circle in the Square") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), directed by Arkin with a cast that included [Linda Lavin](/wiki/Linda_Lavin "Linda Lavin"), [Vincent Gardenia](/wiki/Vincent_Gardenia "Vincent Gardenia"), Elizabeth Wilson and [Fred Willard](/wiki/Fred_Willard "Fred Willard").[[17]](#cite_note-17) Feiffer had no creative involvement in the production.[[13]](#cite_note-atrocious-13) [*The* *New York Times*](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times") called the production "fantastically funny."[[18]](#cite_note-18) That production ran for 400 performances, and won Feiffer an [Obie Award](/wiki/Obie_Award "Obie Award"). Lavin won the 1969 [Outer Critics Circle Award](/wiki/Outer_Critics_Circle_Awards "Outer Critics Circle Awards") for Best Performance.

There was also a successful 1969 production in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles").[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Arkin directed Feiffer's second play *The White House Murder Case*.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Film production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Film production")]

### Jean-Luc Godard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Jean-Luc Godard")]

In January 1969, Elliott Gould announced he had formed his own production company with Jack Brodsky and that they would make two films: *The Assistant*, from [a novel](/wiki/The_Assistant_(Malamud_novel) "The Assistant (Malamud novel)") by [Bernard Malamud](/wiki/Bernard_Malamud "Bernard Malamud"), and *Little Murders*. Gould said he signed [Jean-Luc Godard](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Godard "Jean-Luc Godard") to direct.[[22]](#cite_note-22) [United Artists](/wiki/United_Artists "United Artists") were going to finance and release it, and [Robert Benton](/wiki/Robert_Benton "Robert Benton") and [David Newman](/wiki/David_Newman_(screenwriter) "David Newman (screenwriter)") would write the script.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Gould was unhappy with Benton and Newman's script, and Godard's interest waned.[[24]](#cite_note-arkin-24)

### Alan Arkin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Alan Arkin")]

Feiffer wrote the script. He added new scenes, including new characters such as Alfred Chamberlain's parents (played by [John Randolph](/wiki/John_Randolph_(actor) "John Randolph (actor)") and [Doris Roberts](/wiki/Doris_Roberts "Doris Roberts")). Feiffer said "all I've done in the screenplay is change it from a theatre cliché to a movie cliché."[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Gould asked Arkin to direct (he had never directed a feature before but had directed two short films and had extensive experience as a theatre director). Arkin was reluctant to return to the material but was persuaded after a series of meetings with Feiffer; he was announced as the director in March 1970.[[24]](#cite_note-arkin-24)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) Arkin said the film was "about the human condition."[[30]](#cite_note-30)

[Richard Zanuck](/wiki/Richard_Zanuck "Richard Zanuck"), head of [Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Studios "20th Century Studios"), who had made *[M\*A\*S\*H](/wiki/M*A*S*H_(film) "M*A*S*H (film)")* and *[Move](/wiki/Move_(1970_film) "Move (1970 film)")* with Gould, agreed to finance the film for $1.4 million. Gould's salary was $200,000, deferred until after the film made a profit.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Shooting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Shooting")]

Filming began in April 1970 and finished June 11, $100,000 under budget.[[24]](#cite_note-arkin-24) "Frankly, I'm scared by what we did," said Arkin, "particularly the last 10 minutes."[[24]](#cite_note-arkin-24) Feiffer later said "there were all sorts of problems" with Arkin on the film, "although we had worked together very happily on the play. And it was by no means collaborative. He really wanted nothing to do with me. I had very little input into how that movie came out, and some of it is good and some of it isn't." Feiffer said he was not pleased with the film. "I think that's not his fault, entirely, it's also mine. I made compromises on the screenplay that were not his idea, they were my own. I was inexperienced, and they were dumb ideas. But then there were things that were his fault. Some of his casting. The style of the film, which worked very well on stage, but wasn't appropriate for film, I don't think."[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Release and reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Release and reception")]

### Home media and rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Home media and rights")]

Following its initial release, the film was made available on VHS and DVD, and in 2018, it was released on Blu-ray, with this release including new special features. In the 2000s, the film was also in rotation on the [Fox Movie Channel](/wiki/FX_Movie_Channel "FX Movie Channel"), a channel dedicated to 20th Century Fox Films.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In 2019, [Rupert Murdoch](/wiki/Rupert_Murdoch "Rupert Murdoch") sold most of [21st Century Fox](/wiki/21st_Century_Fox "21st Century Fox")'s film and television assets to [Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company"), and *Little Murders* was one of the films included in the deal.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) Since then, Disney have not made the film available on their streaming services or any other digital platforms.

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Critical response")]

In 1971, the film was given a limited release to allow critical reception to grow.[[24]](#cite_note-arkin-24)

[Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") of the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")* gave the film a perfect four stars and wrote, "One of the reasons it works, and is indeed a definitive reflection of America's darker moods, is that it breaks audiences down into isolated individuals, vulnerable and uncertain. Most movies create a temporary sort of democracy, a community of strangers there in the darkened theater. Not this one. The movie seems to be saying that New York City has a similar effect on its citizens, and that it will get you if you don't watch out."[[36]](#cite_note-36) [Gene Siskel](/wiki/Gene_Siskel "Gene Siskel") of the *[Chicago Tribune](/wiki/Chicago_Tribune "Chicago Tribune")* also awarded his top grade of four stars and called it "a mean little comedy that made me laugh and then think, 'God, how could I laugh at that.'"[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[Roger Greenspun](/wiki/Roger_Greenspun "Roger Greenspun") of *The New York Times* wrote, "Essentially *Little Murders* constructs its world from the point of view of someone sitting behind the locked doors of an apartment on the [Upper West Side](/wiki/Upper_West_Side "Upper West Side")—and so long as it maintains the conditions of that point of view it works—dramatically, cinematically, whatever way you will. Once it breaks with those conditions it becomes unterrifying, unfunny, superficial, inadequate. But *Little Murders* usually is funny—in its great harangues and sermons, in its superlative cast, and in Arkin's direct intelligence in handling most of the dramatic moments."[[38]](#cite_note-38) [Vincent Canby](/wiki/Vincent_Canby "Vincent Canby"), also writing in the *Times*, was more positive, calling it "a very funny, very intelligent, very affecting movie."[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

*[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* wrote, "Combining comedy with deadly serious comment on the nature of the world is a most difficult undertaking. If the theme is violence, and the design is to create a shattering experience for the audience, the project becomes even more difficult. But Alan Arkin, making a most impressive directorial debut, has surmounted these difficulties brilliantly. He has made a film that is not only funny but devastating in its emotional impact."[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[Charles Champlin](/wiki/Charles_Champlin "Charles Champlin") of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* called the film "brilliantly successful" and "a remarkable debut for Arkin as a movie maker."[[43]](#cite_note-43) Gary Arnold of *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* wrote that the film "has good lines and bits of performance (especially by Miss Wilson and Vincent Gardenia as the elder Newquists and [Don Sutherland](/wiki/Donald_Sutherland "Donald Sutherland") as a hippie minister), but it doesn't have a consistent, unifying point of view."[[44]](#cite_note-44)

On [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film had an approval rating of 71% based on reviews from 14 critics.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Little_Murders&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

* On the album *Cowbirds and Cuckoos* by [Ryland Bouchard](/wiki/Ryland_Bouchard "Ryland Bouchard") there is a song titled "Little Murders."
* On the popular [AMC](/wiki/AMC_(TV_channel) "AMC (TV channel)") television show *[Mad Men](/wiki/Mad_Men "Mad Men")*, set in the 1960s, the character of [Megan Draper](/wiki/Megan_Draper "Megan Draper") ([Jessica Paré](/wiki/Jessica_Par%C3%A9 "Jessica Paré")) auditions for the original stage production in the [fifth season](/wiki/Mad_Men_season_5 "Mad Men season 5") episode "[The Other Woman](/wiki/The_Other_Woman_(Mad_Men) "The Other Woman (Mad Men)")".
* [Dave Sim](/wiki/Dave_Sim "Dave Sim") has stated that in his comic book *[Cerebus](/wiki/Cerebus_the_Aardvark "Cerebus the Aardvark")*, the character of The Judge is based on [Lou Jacobi](/wiki/Lou_Jacobi "Lou Jacobi")'s portrayal of Judge Stern from this film.[[46]](#cite_note-46)
